---
title: "Predicting locations for bus infrastructure in Philadelphia County"
subtitle: "To invest/de-invest into bus infrastructure"
author: "Alejandro Duque, Umair Bin Saad, Indrajeet Pruthviraj Chavan, William Rantala  <br> Star Advisors"
date: "Tuesday, April 28, 2026"
format: 
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    code-line-numbers: true
    incremental: false
    smaller: true
    scrollable: true
---



## The Problem

<div style="font-size: 26px; line-height: 1.6;">

**What policy problem are you solving?**
<p style="text-align: justify;">
SEPTA's bus network lacks a data-driven framework to identify which stops 
and routes need infrastructure investment or should be deprioritized — 
leading to inefficient resource allocation.
</p><br>


**Who is affected and why does it matter?**
<p style="text-align: justify;">
Philadelphia residents — especially low-income and transit-dependent 
communities — who rely on buses as their primary mode of transportation. 
Poor infrastructure directly impacts mobility, equity, and economic access.
</p><br>


**What decision would your model inform?**
<p style="text-align: justify;">
Where SEPTA and city planners should allocate budget for bus infrastructure 
upgrades (shelters, frequency, stops) and where services may need redesign 
or reduction.
</p><br>


</div>


## The Solution

<div style="font-size: 23px; line-height: 1.6;">

**What are you predictinsg and why?**  
<p style="text-align: justify;">
We are predicting ridership demand across bus stop locations using 
demographic, spatial, and operational variables — because demand is the 
strongest indicator of where investment is most needed.
</p><br>

**How does prediction help solve the problem?**  
<p style="text-align: justify;">
By quantifying expected ridership at each location, planners can rank stops 
by need, identify underserved high-demand areas, and justify investment 
decisions with evidence rather than assumption.
</p><br>

**What makes your approach better than current practice?**  
<p style="text-align: justify;">
Current decisions are often based on historical patterns or political 
priorities. Our model integrates Census demographics, spatial features, and 
ridership data into a Negative Binomial regression framework — making it 
reproducible, transparent, and equity-aware.
</p><br>

</div>


---

## Data sources



**Data Sources Used:**

- **Primary Dataset:** SEPTA Bus Ridership Data (2022 to 2025)  
- **Secondary Dataset:** Census / ACS Socio-Economic Data /Philadelphia boundary 

<div style="font-size: 18px; line-height: 1.6;">


 Why We Are Combining These Sources

✔ **Explains transit demand patterns more accurately**  
We combine bus ridership data with Census/ACS socio-economic data to better explain why ridership varies across locations.

✔ **Captures who depends on transit and where**  
Demographic variables such as population density, income, race, and vehicle ownership help identify transit-dependent communities.

✔ **Explains spatial differences in ridership**  
These variables improve the model by accounting for neighborhood-level variation beyond bus stop locations alone.

✔ **Spatially joined using buffer-based linkage**  
Data is joined spatially using neighborhood or buffer-based linkage around bus stops, representing local catchment areas (e.g., 400–800m radius).

✔ **Supports equitable transit planning decisions**  
This integration makes the model more realistic, policy-relevant, and useful for guiding infrastructure investment decisions.   
</div>

---


## Methods overview: <br>

<p style="text-align: justify;"> We are using **Count Models (Poisson/negative binomial)** for our this model which "Predict appropriate locations for investment or disinvestment in bus infrastructure in Philadelphia County using bus stop and ridership data." because:<br><br></p>
  
  
 <p style="text-align: justify;"> Since bus ridership and related transit activity are **count data**, Poisson or Negative Binomial regression models are appropriate.<br><br></p>  


<p style="text-align: justify;"> -Linear regression is unsuitable because it assumes continuous data.<br><br></p>

<p style="text-align: justify;"> -Transit ridership data often shows high variation, with some stops being very busy while many others have low usage, leading to uneven distribution. This creates over dispersion (variance greater than the mean), making the Negative Binomial model more suitable than Poisson.</p></p>



---


## Feature engineering: <br>

We are using the following categorical variables in our model:
1. Weekday vs Weekend  
2. High ridership route vs Low ridership route<br>


we are using these variables to:
<p style="text-align: justify;"> 1. Capture differences in travel behavior  
Transit demand changes significantly between weekdays (commuting) and weekends (leisure travel) 

2. Represent route-level demand structure  
 Distinguishes high-demand corridors from low-demand or underperforming routes 
 
3. Improve policy relevance  
 Helps identify where to invest (high ridership) and where to optimize or redesign service (low ridership)  
 
4. Easy interpretation for planning decisions  
 Results can be directly translated into operational and infrastructure planning actions  </p>


---

## Model Evaluation | Spatial Features<br>

**Nearest Transit Stops**
<p style="text-align: justify;">
Distance to nearest transit stops is used as a spatial predictor, capturing 
accessibility and connectivity of bus infrastructure across Philadelphia County.
</p><br>

**Jobs Within a Half Mile (LEHD LODES)**
<p style="text-align: justify;">
Employment density within a 0.5-mile buffer is included to capture the 
influence of job centers on transit demand and ridership patterns.
</p><br>

**Roads**
<p style="text-align: justify;">
Road network data is incorporated to account for physical accessibility 
and connectivity surrounding each bus stop location.
</p><br>

---

## Model Evaluation | Local Hotspots & Cold Spots<br>

**Density of Stops Across Philadelphia**
<p style="text-align: justify;">
Kernel density estimation is applied to identify spatial clusters of high 
and low bus stop concentration — revealing hotspots of transit activity 
and cold spots of underservice across Philadelphia County.
</p>

---

## Model Evaluation | Model Validation

**Cross Validation**
<p style="text-align: justify;">
K-fold cross-validation is applied to assess model generalizability by 
partitioning the data into training and testing sets across multiple folds, 
ensuring the model performs consistently beyond the training data.
</p>

**Accuracy Metrics — MAE (Mean Absolute Error)**
<p style="text-align: justify;">
Mean Absolute Error (MAE) is used to measure the average magnitude of 
prediction errors, providing an interpretable and policy-relevant measure 
of model accuracy in predicting ridership counts.
</p>

---



## The Evidence | Model Accuracy

**How accurate is your model?**

- MAE of **[X] riders per stop** — predictions deviate by an average of 
[X] passengers
- Model explains approximately **[X]%** of variation in ridership across 
Philadelphia County
- Overall predictive performance is **[strong/moderate]**

---

## The Evidence | Model Validation

**How did you validate it?**

- Applied **k-fold cross-validation** — dataset split into training and 
testing sets across [k] folds
- Consistent MAE scores averaging **[X]** across folds — confirms model 
generalizes well
- No evidence of **overfitting** to specific locations

**Spatial Autocorrelation Diagnostics**

- Moran's I on residuals = **[X]** (p-value = [X])
- Indicates spatial autocorrelation is **[negligible/partially addressed]**
- Model **[adequately captures/does not fully capture]** spatial structure 
of ridership demand

---

## The Evidence | Limitations

**What are its limitations?**

- Relies on **static Census/ACS data** — may not reflect recent demographic 
shifts
- Ridership counts are **aggregated at stop level** — do not capture trip 
purpose or time-of-day variation
- **Weekday/weekend** distinction is the only temporal control included
- Does not account for **real-time service disruptions** or recent SEPTA 
network changes

---

## The Evidence | Bias Considerations

**Could it introduce or perpetuate bias?**

- Model uses **race and income** as predictors — risk of reinforcing 
existing inequities
- Low ridership may reflect **poor service quality** rather than low demand
- Disinvestment decisions could **disproportionately impact** low-income 
and minority communities
- Model outputs must be interpreted alongside **equity considerations** 
to ensure fair infrastructure planning

---


## {.flexbox .vcenter}

<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: 20%;">

<p style="font-size: 100px; font-weight: bold;">
Recommendations
</p>

</div>

---

## The Implementation | Practical Use

**How would the city actually use this?**

- Model outputs can be used to generate an **investment priority map** 
of Philadelphia County bus stops
- Planners can rank stops by **predicted ridership demand** to guide 
budget allocation
- High-demand locations flagged for **infrastructure upgrades** — shelters, 
frequency, accessibility improvements
- Low-demand locations flagged for **service redesign** or operational 
optimization
- Can be integrated into **SEPTA's capital planning cycle** as an 
evidence-based decision support tool

---

## The Implementation | Data Requirements

**What data would they need to collect?**

- **Ridership data** — regular updates to SEPTA boarding/alighting counts 
by stop
- **Census/ACS updates** — annual demographic data at census tract level
- **Land use & employment data** — updated LEHD LODES jobs data within 
catchment areas
- **Road network data** — OpenStreetMap or PennDOT road updates
- **Infrastructure condition data** — current state of bus stops, shelters, 
and amenities
- **Real-time service data** — disruptions, route changes, and frequency 
adjustments

---

## The Implementation | Costs & Benefits

**What are the costs?**

- **Data collection & maintenance** — ongoing updates to ridership and 
census data
- **Technical capacity** — GIS and statistical expertise required to run 
and update the model
- **Integration costs** — embedding model into existing SEPTA planning 
workflows

**What are the benefits?**

- **Efficient resource allocation** — directs limited infrastructure 
budgets to highest-need locations
- **Evidence-based decisions** — reduces reliance on political priorities 
or ad hoc planning
- **Equity gains** — identifies underserved transit-dependent communities 
for targeted investment
- **Long-term cost savings** — prevents over-investment in low-demand 
locations

---

## The Implementation | Safeguards Needed

**What safeguards are needed?**

- **Equity review** — model outputs must be reviewed against equity 
metrics before any disinvestment decision
- **Community engagement** — affected neighborhoods should be consulted, 
especially low-income and minority communities
- **Transparency** — model methodology and results should be publicly 
accessible and explainable
- **Regular model updates** — predictions should be refreshed with new 
data to remain accurate and relevant
- **Human oversight** — final investment decisions should involve planners, 
not be fully automated by the model
- **Bias audits** — periodic checks to ensure model does not systematically 
disadvantage vulnerable communities

---

## {.flexbox .vcenter}

<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: 20%;">

<p style="font-size: 60px; font-weight: bold;">
Any Questions & Answers
</p>

<br>

<p style="font-size: 36px; font-style: italic;">
Thank you for your attention!
</p>

</div>
